title: Cookware and Equipment
content:
EQUIPMENT & UTENSILS

SELECTION PRINCIPLES

Choose fairly heavy gauge—not so heavy as to be unwieldy, but heavy enough to diffuse heat evenly. Avoid thin pans that develop hot spots and cause sticking or require excessive stirring.

PAN MATERIALS

Heavy Aluminum: Good diffusion; will pit and discolor. Clean with cream of tartar solution (2 teaspoons per quart water boiled 5-10 minutes).

Copper: Best in heavier gauges; quick, even heat distribution. Must be well-tinned or stainless-lined on food-contact surfaces (affected by acids; can be poisonous). Clean with vinegar-salt mixture.

Stainless Steel: Easiest to clean; poor conductivity offset by thin gauge (hot spots develop; food burns easily). Best with inner core of aluminum or copper for increased heat diffusion.

Iron: Heavy but low conductivity; rusts easily; discolors acid foods. Season new skillets: wash, dry, coat with unsalted oil, wipe before use. Reseason old: scour, wash with soap (not detergent), dry, coat with shortening, bake 350°F 2 hours.

Tempered Glass/Porcelain Enamel: Poor heat conductors. Glass apt to crack; enamel scratches and chips. Unless high quality and acid-resistant, glaze affected by acids. High heat may make enamel stick to heating element—use trivet.

Earthenware: Poor conductor but holds heat well; doesn't discolor foods. Heavy; breaks with sudden temperature changes. Some danger of lead transference from soft glazes. Season unglazed casseroles: rub with garlic, fill with boiling water and onion skins/celery/leek tops; boil/simmer 4 hours; wash.

Tinware: Good conductivity; mars easily; rusts quickly; darkens after use; affected by acid foods.

Greaseless Pans: Soapstone griddle; newer silicone/fluorocarbon surfaces withstand temperatures to 450°F. Do not affect heat distribution; prevent sticking. Scratch easily—use nylon/wooden utensils.

Plastics: Some withstand relatively high heat but not for cooking or containing hot liquids that may dissolve plastic and cause burns. Many storage containers cannot be washed in water over 140°F. Surfaces retain grease—do not use for whipping egg whites.

Combination Materials: Look for good flat bottoms made of alloys combining superior diffusion of aluminum, quick conductivity of copper, and non-corrodible quality of stainless steel.

COOKWARE GUIDELINES

- Fit pan to size of heat unit for better results and fuel economy
- Ensure tight-fitting lid if process calls for one
- Appropriate pan size to contents vital (especially braising, baking)
- Round pans give more even browning; square pans brown heavier at corners
- Shiny metal deflects heat; dark enamel or glass catches and holds heat—reduce oven temperatures 25°F when using
- In baking, fill pans no more than ⅔ full for cakes to reach top

BAKING PAN PLACEMENT

- Place racks before heating oven
- For browning sauce or gratineed casserole: briefly under broiler
- Overcrowding results in uneven baking—allow 2 inches between pans and walls
- Stagger pans if using two shelves
- Cake position: just above center generally; below center for angel food, torten, soufflés (prevents hard surface from top element)
- Add moisture if required: shallow pan of water on oven floor

THERMOSTATS

Set to desired temperature; preheat 10-15 minutes if no indicator light. Insert pans quickly; minimize peeking. Do not set higher than specified to speed heating—will not improve results and may throw thermostat out of gear. Thermostats need adjustment at least annually.

CLEANING

Clean oven maintains temperature and reflects heat more accurately than untidy one. Self-cleaning ovens available.

Scorched pots: First plunge into cold water (lessens scorched taste). Transfer food to clean container. Clean scorched pot: nylon pouf or brush with built-in detergent; or soak overnight with detergent. If insufficient: boil 1 teaspoon washing soda or cream of tartar per quart water in scorched pot.
